sections.passage:
ABOUT DRAGNET JOB APTITUDE TEST
The test section measures your verbal reasoning ability, logical reasoning and problem solving ability. This material provides in-depth insight into how the test looked like in the past.
BREAKDOWN:
20-NUMERICAL REASONING QUESTIONS TIME=20MINUTES
20-VERBAL REASONING QUESTIONS TIME=20MINUTES
10-16 ABSTRACT (INDUCTIVE) REASONING TIME=10MINUTES
TEST SCORING SYSTEM
The questions in this test all carry equal marks and no negative marking is applied. However, it is advisable that you double-check your answer choices.
USE OF CALCULATOR
Use of calculators are not allowed, but the test administrator is at liberty to decide whether or not use of calculators will be permitted in the hall. So it is best you factor in this reality, and try to practice without using calculator.
STUDY PACK NAVIGATION:
Use the in-built navigation system (at the left side of this study pack) to quickly go to any section you want to study.
RECOMMENDATION:
Please ensure that you go through all questions provided in this study pack to get a well rounded preparation. All questions from Dragnet's past entry level job aptitude test. Happy Studying!

questions.content: The “ground-up” and “trees-down” theories are the only theories explaining flight in birds.
questions.explanation: Although the 'ground-up' and 'trees-down' theories are stated to be theories of flight in birds, they are not stated to be the only two theories explaining flight in birds.

questions.content: All dinosaurs had hollow bones.
questions.explanation: The passage states that other feathered dinosaurs also had hollow bones, not that all dinosaurs (non-feathered) also had hollow bones.

questions.content: There is no scientific consensus regarding how birds evolved powered flight.
questions.explanation: The passage states that further research is required to conclusively verify the process in which birds evolved powered flight, and that the evolution of powered flight in birds is still debated.

questions.content: The dinosaurian origins of birds is widely rejected.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that the dinosaurian origins of birds is 'largely undisputed', so it is not widely rejected.

questions.content: Both stray and feral cats exhibit hostility when first encountering humans.
questions.explanation: The passage distinguishes stray from feral cats by noting that 'unlike strays, feral cats initially show hostility towards humans.'

questions.content: Biodiversity can be affected by feral cat populations.
questions.explanation: The passage states that feral cats can become invasive, resulting in a decline in biodiversity.

questions.content: Feral cats are rarely preyed upon.
questions.explanation: Although non-indigenous feral cats have few predators, the passage does not state that all feral cats have few predators.

questions.content: Domestic cats can be raised in the wild.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that feral cats are domestic cats that have been raised in the wild.

questions.content: The destruction of property has no effect on the economy.
questions.explanation: The glazier’s fallacy is used to highlight that destruction of property does affect economic activity.

questions.content: Society does not benefit from the cost of repairing destroyed property.
questions.explanation: The passage states that society does not benefit from the money spent on repairing destroyed property.

questions.content: The destruction of property negatively impacts the economy.
questions.explanation: The passage only indicates that money spent on repairs does not benefit society, not that destruction itself negatively impacts the economy.

questions.content: Repairing broken windows results in job creation.
questions.explanation: The passage suggests that repairing windows may provide employment, but this is uncertain.

questions.content: Excessive saving has no impact on economic growth.
questions.explanation: The passage states that excessive saving lowers aggregate demand, leading to reduced economic growth.

questions.content: Excessive saving has no impact on savers themselves.
questions.explanation: Excessive saving is noted to negatively impact both savers and the economy.

questions.content: Saving money negatively impacts the economy.
questions.explanation: The passage distinguishes between excessive saving and regular saving; it does not state the impact of regular saving on the economy.

questions.content: Excess saving has no effect on aggregate demand.
questions.explanation: The passage indicates that reduced spending due to excessive saving causes aggregate demand to fall.

questions.content: Artificial intelligences perform advanced reasoning more effectively than humans.
questions.explanation: The passage does not provide enough information to determine whether artificial intelligences perform advanced reasoning more effectively than humans.

questions.content: Playing chess is a complex activity among humans.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that chess is considered complex by human standards.

questions.content: Simple artificial intelligences cannot recognise faces.
questions.explanation: According to the passage, only the most advanced artificial intelligences can recognise faces, so simple AIs cannot.

questions.content: Science fiction does not feature artificial intelligences.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that artificial intelligences are commonly featured in science fiction.

sections.passage: Work-related stress is one of the biggest causes of sick leave in the UK. The passage describes various causes of stress at work including poor working conditions, long hours, relationship problems with colleagues, or lack of job security. It also details the physical and emotional problems that can result from stress, such as headaches, tiredness, lack of concentration, and more. It then presents statistics from a study where one in six of the UK working population said their job is very stressful, and other details about the impact of stress on work and life.

questions.content: Stress at work is often caused by relationship problems with your partner.
questions.explanation: The passage refers only to relationship problems with colleagues, not with a partner.

questions.content: Work-related stress can result in tiredness and a lack of concentration.
questions.explanation: The passage lists tiredness and lack of concentration as effects of work-related stress.

questions.content: One in six working men say their job is very stressful.
questions.explanation: The statistic refers to one in six of the UK working population, not specifically working men.

questions.content: If you spend more time with your family, you will not suffer from stress.
questions.explanation: The passage indicates that lack of family time may cause stress but does not state that more time will definitively prevent stress.

questions.content: Photothermal Ablation is the latest breakthrough in chemical treatment for cancer.
questions.explanation: The passage states that Photothermal Ablation is a non-chemical procedure used for treating cancerous tumours.

questions.content: Nanoparticles are objects whose dimensions are measured in nanometres, or billionths of a metre.
questions.explanation: The passage does not give the specific dimensions of nanoparticles.

questions.content: Nanoparticles can absorb infrared light.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that nanoparticles can absorb infrared light.

questions.content: Greg Wyler had a background in telecoms.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that Greg Wyler is a former owner of Rwanda’s national telephone company.

questions.content: The satellites for the project will cost €8m each.
questions.explanation: The passage does not provide enough information to determine the cost per satellite.

questions.content: The majority of developing countries lie within 100km of the equator.
questions.explanation: The passage states that services will be available in a 100km corridor around the equator, which covers most developing countries.

questions.content: Bullying in the workplace hinders UK economic output.
questions.explanation: The passage states that workplace bullying results in significant lost output and costs.

questions.content: Another name for workplace bullying is coercive management.
questions.explanation: The passage indicates that coercive management is used as a euphemism for bullying.

questions.content: Bullying in the workplace is sometimes caused by religious intolerance.
questions.explanation: While the passage notes race is mentioned in bullying, it does not explicitly state that religious intolerance is a cause.

questions.content: Deliberately ignoring a colleague is a form of bullying.
questions.explanation: The passage states that deliberately ignoring a fellow worker is considered a form of bullying.

questions.content: The Phoenix Mars Lander has provided proof that life once existed on Mars.
questions.explanation: The passage reports discoveries and speculations but does not state that proof of past life has been provided.

questions.content: Life forms on Mars require water in order to survive.
questions.explanation: The passage does not provide enough evidence to conclude that all life forms on Mars require water.

questions.content: Since the Phoenix Mars Lander cannot excavate it is limited to surface photography.
questions.explanation: The passage indicates that the Lander dug a ditch, showing it is capable of excavation.

sections.passage: A passage discussing common difficulties in business management is provided. It explains that when a team member’s behavior conflicts with established practices, an effective approach is to lead by example and invoke mission statement values. The passage also discusses how understanding individual communication styles is important for managing teams, as well as the benefits of a flexible approach, effective PR, and fostering entrepreneurial spirit among employees.

questions.content: Leading by example is an effective approach in dealing with problematic behaviour from employees.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that leading by example is effective.

questions.content: A manager who understands how people communicate is able to take a flexible approach in dealing with problems.
questions.explanation: While both effective communication and flexibility are mentioned, the passage does not directly state that one implies the other.

questions.content: In a good team, the needs of the organisation are secondary to the needs of the individual.
questions.explanation: The passage states explicitly that a good team always puts the needs of the organisation first.

questions.content: The best approach to PR is to be flexible, regularly changing the company's goals to keep the public interested.
questions.explanation: The passage stresses consistency in PR messaging rather than constantly changing the goals.

questions.content: Not disclosing a full company history to the PR officer will result in a failed campaign.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes the importance of disclosure but does not claim that lack of disclosure will definitely cause failure.

questions.content: It is recommended to wait before taking advantage of any new opportunities to ensure they are beneficial.
questions.explanation: The passage advises seizing opportunities as they arise rather than waiting.

questions.content: Employees instilled with the idea of personal entrepreneurship will be less willing to contribute to the success of the company as a whole.
questions.explanation: The passage states that such employees will work as willing partners in the company's success.

questions.content: New staff members can be indoctrinated with the virtues of entrepreneurship.
questions.explanation: The passage indicates that orientation and value-formation training can induce entrepreneurial thinking.

questions.content: Employees encouraged to think of themselves as entrepreneurs work fewer hours than other staff members.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that entrepreneurial employees tend to work long hours, but it does not compare them explicitly to others.

questions.content: Employees must reject criticism to build a good working relationship with their boss.
questions.explanation: The passage states that accepting criticism gracefully helps build a good working relationship.

questions.content: A bad relationship with a boss can lead to missed opportunities, but does not risk an employee's reputation.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that a bad relationship can damage professional reputations.

questions.content: Flattering the boss can be an effective approach for an employee seeking promotion.
questions.explanation: The passage comments on artificial flattery being ineffective but does not fully address sincere flattery.

questions.content: Knowledge of a company's reputation is not useful for salespeople.
questions.explanation: The passage clearly states that knowledge of a company's reputation is useful for salespeople.

questions.content: The personal traits and abilities of a company's personnel can influence the confidence people have in their product.
questions.explanation: The passage confirms that product confidence is partly based on confidence in the personnel.

questions.content: It is helpful to have knowledge of the background, policies and reputation of a company when developing an effective sales pitch.
questions.explanation: The passage supports that detailed company knowledge can help form an effective sales pitch.

sections.passage: The passage begins by clarifying that open-source software should not be confused with freeware. It explains that while most open-source software is free, it must be released under an open-source license and allow modifications. It then contrasts this with commercial software that protects its source code and provides additional support. Following this, another passage describes the Ring of Fire—the area encircling the Pacific basin with high seismic and volcanic activity—and explains the geological process of subduction. Lastly, a passage on whaling details the history of whale hunting, the role of the International Whaling Commission, and debates over the whaling ban.

questions.content: Open-source software is free, and is not released under a license.
questions.explanation: The passage states that open-source software is available free but is released under an open-source license.

questions.content: Because it is developed collaboratively, open-source software is of better quality than proprietary software.
questions.explanation: The passage presents arguments both for and against the quality of open-source software.

questions.content: One claimed advantage of open-source software over licenced software is greater flexibility.
questions.explanation: The passage states that open-source software can be easily adapted to users’ needs.

questions.content: Technical support is not available for open-source software.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that developers charge for installation, training, and technical support for open-source software.

questions.content: It is prohibited to modify free open-source software and then license it for a fee.
questions.explanation: The passage states that any modifications must be distributed under the same terms as the original software.

questions.content: Mexico is located along the eastern side of the Ring of Fire.
questions.explanation: While Mexico is part of the Ring of Fire, the passage does not explicitly include this fact.

questions.content: Subduction occurs whenever two tectonic plates move in opposite directions.
questions.explanation: The passage defines subduction as occurring when adjacent plates converge and one slides under the other, not merely when they move in opposite directions.

questions.content: There are no volcanoes further south than Mount Erebus.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that Mount Erebus is the world’s southern-most active volcano, but does not address whether dormant volcanoes exist further south.

questions.content: Molten rock rises during a volcanic eruption.
questions.explanation: The passage states that molten rock rises to the surface and erupts during a volcanic eruption.

questions.content: The world’s most severe earthquakes and volcanic eruptions occur within the Ring of Fire.
questions.explanation: Although most earthquakes and volcanoes occur in the Ring of Fire, the passage does not claim that the most severe events occur there.

questions.content: The International Whaling Commission is a regulatory organisation covering both commercial and environmental interests.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the IWC was established to ensure whale conservation and oversee whaling industry development.

questions.content: Industrialisation led to reduced demand for whale oil in the twentieth century.
questions.explanation: The passage implies a change after industrialisation but does not explicitly state that demand was reduced.

questions.content: Japan, Norway and Iceland are the only nations in favour of repealing the 1986 whaling moratorium.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions these nations engage in whaling but does not state they are the only ones seeking repeal.

questions.content: The whaling ban has resulted in the recovery of all whaling populations.
questions.explanation: Although the ban has led to some recovery, the passage also notes that whales remain vulnerable.

questions.content: It is arguable that whales are more valuable alive than dead, even in nations where whale meat is eaten.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions whale-watching as a profitable industry but does not compare its value directly to that of whaling.

sections.passage: The first passage in this section discusses oil sands. It explains that oil sands are most commonly found in Venezuela’s Orinoco Basin and Alberta, Canada, and describes the differences between crude oil and the viscous bitumen obtained from oil sands. It also highlights environmental impacts and economic factors such as greenhouse gas emissions and profitability. A second passage discusses Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (CFS), its symptoms, lack of consensus on its causes, and the controversy over its naming and research findings.

questions.content: Oil sands offer a clean solution for meeting future energy needs.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes environmental pollution caused by oil sands development.

questions.content: Oil sands are only found in Alberta and the Orinoco Basin.
questions.explanation: The passage says they are 'most commonly' found in these regions, implying they can occur elsewhere.

questions.content: Bitumen is a highly viscous form of petroleum that needs to be heated to flow.
questions.explanation: The passage only states that bitumen is highly viscous and does not mention the effect of heating.

questions.content: It is almost as profitable to extract conventional oil as oil sands.
questions.explanation: The passage infers that rising demand and higher petrol prices have made oil sands extraction nearly as economically viable as conventional oil.

questions.content: Extracting bitumen from conventional oil generates four times the level of greenhouse gases than extracting from oil sands.
questions.explanation: The passage states the reverse – that bitumen extraction from oil sands produces four times more greenhouse gases.

questions.content: There is a lack of consensus within the medical community about CFS’s symptoms.
questions.explanation: The passage details controversies about CFS but does not state if the symptoms are debated.

questions.content: Many patients believe the name Myalgic Encephalomyelitis trivialises the condition.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions objections to the name CFS but does not specify views on the alternative name.

questions.content: A 2009 study linking CFS with a retrovirus has now been discredited.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the link was disproven by further research.

questions.content: The symptoms of Chronic Fatigue Syndrome are also attributable to other illnesses.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that doctors must rule out other conditions with similar symptoms.

questions.content: CFS is a severely debilitating condition for 17 million people worldwide.
questions.explanation: While the passage gives an estimated number of sufferers, it does not specify how many have a severely debilitating form.

sections.passage: This section’s passage discusses dreaming. It explains that there is no unifying theory for dreaming, describes the REM sleep state (including muscle paralysis and the typical sleep cycle), and introduces the scanning hypothesis about eye movements during REM sleep. It also notes that the complexity of a child’s dreams increases with age.

questions.content: REM sleep tends to be preceded by non-REM sleep.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the most common sleeping pattern is for REM sleep to be preceded by four stages of non-REM sleep.

questions.content: Eye muscles are the only muscles that are not paralysed during REM sleep.
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that most body muscles are paralysed and only specifies that eye muscles are not, but does not rule out other muscles.

questions.content: REM sleep periods always occur after four non-REM sleep periods.
questions.explanation: The passage states that this is the most common pattern but not an absolute rule.

questions.content: The scanning hypothesis states that the direction of a lucid dreamer’s eye movements reveals what the dream is about.
questions.explanation: The hypothesis posits that eyes move in accordance with the direction of gaze in the dream, but this does not reveal the dream’s subject.

questions.content: A child’s dreams may become more sophisticated as their imagination develops.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the potential complexity of a child’s dreams develops as they age alongside their imagination.

sections.passage: The final section discusses the merits of single-sex education in the United States. It explains that while single-sex schooling has its advocates (citing benefits like higher self-esteem and improved class participation), there is also debate about its overall effectiveness. The passage touches on historical legal changes (such as Title IV amendments) and mentions research findings that suggest differences in educational outcomes.

questions.content: Girls who attend single-sex schools perform better in maths and sciences than their counterparts in co-educational schools.
questions.explanation: The passage presents arguments on both sides without definitively stating that girls perform better in maths and sciences.

questions.content: The increased demand for American single-sex state education is a relatively recent phenomenon.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that demand is now on the rise and that single-sex state schools were previously illegal until the 2006 amendment.

questions.content: Educators can tailor their tuition to reflect current research about gender-based brain development.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly mentions that tailoring tuition based on gender-based research is possible.
